speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He bought much of a rustler, you know. He thought he was a new, something you shouldn't tell him. His father was working with my crew. I had a crew working with the Clay Company. He said, you know, it was maybe we could put that son of a bitch and come home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what he done?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He come right over there too.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: You old guy, it's too easy to tell us and swear.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, is that all right?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Let her stay home. I told my husband, Bill, you've got a pair of shoes there. You sure as hell can handle him. He didn't say no more. He clicked on the horse and drove on.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: One of the things I wanted to ask you about was Hayes getting shot. What you remember about Hayes, what you heard about Hayes getting shot. They were policemen, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you hear about that?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, it was all hearsay what I got. But the thing is, both men, before they killed each other, they had to have ingredients against each other. They were both hot -headed. So the story is that Hayes went over there, and they was enjoying themselves, a little family scrap, you know. They were probably with his, had their children with them, and Hayes came over there. And I don't know, so she told her, I suppose, that she met him at the door and told them to go away. They didn't want him there. I mean, he pushed on in anyway, and a rifle bullet cut each other in veins. He emptied his six children in the house, and then he made it back as far as the railroad track there at the depot, and he died there. Then the Hayes boys, they began shooting up there at Sly's house, and of course, he was shooting back, and there used to be, in that sign board of mine at the hotel, some of his boats was in that board where he was shooting at the Hayes boys. I don't know if they were still there or not. I don't think the sign is there anymore. Possibly, no. Oh, not in all the Sears.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: But it was way over 15 years ago, for my time there, and we were very way over 50 years, so that was 60 years or so, you know. Well, what, they got him to give up.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: They got Sly to give up. Oh, yes. He went down a couple, three years, and boys can get married down there and learn to trade what is independent and dream. He never came back and kept a visit. You know, I've heard one story. I don't know if you ever heard this, but I heard a story that Hayes had some friends. He was an odd fellow and had some friends that wanted to lynch Sly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear that? Wanted to do what?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Lynch Sly. Lynch him. No, I hadn't heard that. And I wasn't a member there at that time. I was afterwards. But I don't think there was any members in it that would be inclined to do that. No. No, man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, with this Hayes, he wasn't very well liked, was he?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, in a sense, no. He wasn't a popular man. He was a, he was a good marshal all right. He, he had anything to call for his boy. He'd done it. But he wasn't very popular. His boys wanted to be there. He was, well, he was honest. The boys were crooks. Poker players and crooks. A little fellow farmed. The farm was out near the, just this side of the Burnt Creek Cemetery there.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: The road was right through the old house down on the main, the chain of the road. He was on the farm, then this Henry. He played poker and run a barbershop in town there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why wasn't they liked? Why wasn't they liked?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, they were dirty, underhanded sort of guys, you know. The old man was, but he was honest. He wasn't liked, but he was honest and above board with everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why wasn't that he didn't like Hayes? Was it because he was too hard on the people? Too strict or what was the story?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, I don't know if he hurt people that, other people complain on. If he was disagreeing with the law, why he'd put you straight on it pretty quick. But I guess if he didn't, he'd let you know. I never knew. I never heard of him imposing on anybody that way. There was one fellow, I feel strong, he ordered him out of town. And I don't know, I feel like I'm kind of sassy about it. You get on the horse, you get the rope. I guess he told me, shoot him if he didn't get it out of town. And it is now, old man, don't you for damn never ever tried that. And he got his rope and good. I don't know, I don't believe that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he rope Hayes?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah, he probably would too. Now I don't think that Hayes was afraid of him. Because that kind of a man, it doesn't scare you know. I think that if he'd really been afraid of him, or if he really wanted to. But it must be that he had some regards for the guy. He didn't want him actually. And another time, the nigger Joe, he ordered him out of town. Well, the nigger got on his horse in the wind, but he took out the sidewalk. And he began shooting at the other side too. Joe, he didn't make town for quite a while. I don't know what he did. He wanted to show us the skier. Go on, see, can't hit a nigger in the dark.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He's a tad, yeah. Getting up on that sidewalk and just showing contempt for the marshal. And the bottom of a horse broke through there and then the bottom broke his leg and maybe it broke the nigger's neck. Of course, the drunken man doesn't give too many things consideration. But I want to mention another case. Here a few years ago, he finally come up from Lewiston. I guess he was hunting, I don't know, but he had a high -fire rifle with him. And he should be down on a steep hill to decide a deer to be on a very quick there, you know. You know, he'd be down on a steep hill. Over in there, he was hunting over in there. He was entertaining himself by supposed shooting up these insulators on the power line. On the dam line, broke the nigger and warned him right there.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Killed him?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: you don't know Bob Smith and Jerry, do you?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I don't think I do. But there'd be several people, anybody there, this is a reason. I don't think he was... that guy wasn't hunting. He was just one of these goes along with it, really. Well, he must have, well, probably he wouldn't, but if he shoot insulators, he could do that down near town. Well, yeah. He must have been hunting. Well, could have been. But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sort of came back on him, didn't it?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah, a wire just... Under the sun, because he's...

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: ...flow around and come on to him and cook you.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Or... huh.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Imagine he's having a lot of fun and shooting at the inside town. But people in dairy, yeah.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Because any amount of people in dairy would recollect that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they know that it was sly that shot Hayes? Was it sly, was it pain sly that shot him?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He was sure that he did it.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, he was shot in that house, yeah. Some... some claim that she'd done it. And of course, she was there and talked to the marshal and... ...and cut his gender veins. But the sly's killed him all right, whichever one done it.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, you know, now, he was... He was asking for it. He had no business. After he was ordered not to come in there, they didn't send for him. And he had no business. And then she...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...putting in there?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: ...telling him to keep away. If she did, of course, that's their story, too. Whatever went on, talk between them. Nobody knows except them. I heard that she tried to slip sly a gun. When he was in... when he was being... waiting to be taken to DeMosco. I don't know if that's true either, but somebody told me that once. Oh, yeah, I heard that he was going to kill right in the door, just the end of the door. No, I mean when sly was in jail. Before he got took to Moscow. Oh, yeah. That'd be possible. If she could get to the jail. But I've heard that the Hayes boys were not liked at all. I've heard they were... They were dirty. No, they trucked them folk with memes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they stay around for long after Hayes?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, yeah, a long time after that. I don't know what the reason is. I guess they went home setting. One was out somewhere in the dust country and then the other fella wound up in Tacoma, I think. But granny, she stayed there and she died. She used to be around town. They walked around with a stick.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I guess I must have seen her, but... Oh, yeah. It seems to me like she's quite a heavy woman.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: She was not quite as heavy as you and about you were not linked. Hey, once again. I heard that the daughter went bad. Yeah, I heard that too. She married a dentist. And then they split up. I heard that about her. I don't know anything about her though. I've seen her a couple of times. She didn't come often, but I saw her for a couple of times. And she's a good looking woman too.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: It's funny about some of those old timers. I can see them just as though they're standing before me right now. They remember so well. And others I just can't remember. Of course, I've never seen her so much as a lot of the other people, but somehow I just don't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see Joe Wells? Did you know Joe Wells at all? Or was that before?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I've seen him, the Negro you mean. Yeah. I've seen him now. I never heard him talk or anything like that.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I just didn't see him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know him at all, Frank?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, by sight, that's all. I never got a parent of him. I think it's good for you and Joe. Of course, he didn't have a whole lot to do with it. He was dead. But it would let me come in with a Troy Lumber camp up there. He had a cute gown, so he could visit. And of course, he unloaded it there. And the nigger had died. I don't know. It was a week or a month, all of that. And they got around, so I started to joke, you know, that these folks would kill the nigger. A couple of the boys in camp, they drank a little bit too much, and they got sick from it. They come in on Saturday. On Monday, these fellas didn't go out to work. They were sitting there after Saturday. One fella, Carlo Johnson, his family's all taking me to the city. He looked over at the other guy. He said, well, you... They say he killed a nigger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he come go down and begin you and me to work?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I thought that was a very well -timed cartoon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think putting up with that noise and worse than that, paid over $500 for it?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: $550. $550. Some of these are real noisy. I've heard them as bad as this. It's bad, though. Yeah.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Keeps the house warm. I'll say that for it.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, we like the old ones in there much better. This is automatic, which is not handy and all of that. If the money goes off, we moved off of that into town before we finally, after a few months, moved up to Helmer. That's when they had passenger trains.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, how many trains a day was it that the freight and passenger trains are just trains going going a little while, you know? You mean a Troy?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah. I was to two passenger trains both weeks daily. And then the freight. And especially at night, you know, the freight. Well, there would be the passenger too at night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You hear the noise a lot?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, it was awful noisy. And we knew that, you know, because it was noisy going up the canyon that we could hear it and clear on up before it got up on top. And we were still on the ranch. And so we thought, oh, that would be awful. That awful noise down there. And people said, ah, I don't bother. Oh, I said, for a week or so, for a while, you'll never hear it. Well, you couldn't believe that, you know. But there was always no time. We never heard the train.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People lived down along the railroad tracks and said, well, unless, is that with us at first?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: We thought, oh, we never can live here. That noise going on all the time. Trains and trains and the west sort of going on. And said, now they don't bother us. But we just sleep right away. We never hear them. And we was along the track. We used to pretty much on the hill, up on top of the hill pretty much. But it was plenty noisy. But it was no time for all the bother. They didn't bother us. So you get used to things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make Troy a very lively town, having the train in there?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Ooh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did having the trains coming in make a big difference, make Troy a lively town to be in?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, no difference. Didn't have much effect on town.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, no difference. No, I wouldn't think the trains would have... But what put the life into Troy in the first place was when that potlatch train railroad went in over there. And then that, and another thing helped at the same time, this being, you know, a trustee used to be here west of Troy. It's a field now. You know, he's right. When you pass the log cabin on your left, come to Moscow, it's over to the right that there was a big trust there. Hell, they had a crew there for six months, I guess, filling that. They put that cut in. Then up now, they underpassed. Before that, the railroad went around the hill there. So they put in that cut and made it for field out there. There was a lot of work in doing that. Oh, God, it was a big crew there. Two work trains there all winter. And then they had it for water. They had to fight along the way beyond the howl and over and under that creek. Must have been a couple of miles or more away. Speaking of, Al -Moshka wouldn't want to get to potlatch mill years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What the devil would they do for water?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No water here. No water here for anything like that. I'll show you how little they knew about it. The same thing as that thing. We had for the Lewis and Mill one then. Oh, everybody in the mill was going to go in and we had up there. We'll hail. They didn't have enough water to do their laundry up there. They were so sure of it. You double camp, put in there, you know. Never showed up.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: You know, nothing about if railroads make a town. I can tell you one that the case of it, when we were kids going to school, we lived in a little town 10 miles out of Grangeville, about 10 miles out of Cuckoo, and lived right between the two, and it was named Denver. And it was quite a little town. A lot of people lived there and there were a lot of business places, even out of bank. Three big hotels, a bank, a saloon. Pool hall, and they had the saloon, and post office, and... Churches. Restaurants, and churches, and it was quite a booming little place. Had a water work, city water work. A lot of people lived there. Well, they were coming in with a railroad from Lewis to Knuth, you know, come across the prairie. Didn't have any railroad up at that time. Had to be freighted by, you know, horses and wagon. And there wasn't trucks in those days either. And very... Well, at that time nobody had cars, so it was horses. And so then this... This would have been a railroad then. Well, they considered bringing them through Denver there. And that might have done something for the little town. But it still... That probably changed, and they went... Bypassed it, went over... I don't know what's north of it. Anyway, four miles away from Denver. Well, poor little Denver just died out, finally. But you know that... And that was a nice, real nice place, a fan, which is still there with trees up it. Railroad station -like. And a real nice place to build a town, but it never built up at all. It just didn't...

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: It was off the beaten path.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, they put the railroad right through. Oh, fan. Fan, yeah. But they didn't take it to Denver. And if they had put that railroad through Denver, it probably would have soon been a pretty good little city.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, I don't know. You see, Cottonwood never made too much of a town. That was a trouble with Denver and... Or fan. Cottonwood was closed here, and Gray was closed here. Was that country...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did that compare to this country? When you came here, what did you think of how the two of them were, compared to each other?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, just the same. Much the same kind of country. Oh, yes.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Lots of winter, lots of snow, lots of wind, rowing, and living.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: The winter really milder winter there than here, though, upon the Camas Prairie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: The winter really monitored it, because last time you'd have buried ground there where we didn't begin to... I guess it's not so high. I'm talking about Troy not here. But it's very much the same. To me, it made no difference when I come to Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that country about to settle up, as this country was at the time?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah, just about the same thing. Well, yes, it was at that time, but now they've combined ranches there, and now there's just a few real large ranches rather than there isn't so many homes there across the prairie. But it's a big prairie, just like, really, this is a big prairie, you know. It stretches out each way, way out, and it makes a big prairie. Our oldest daughter from Montana was called in. They was talking about... We figured out how the difference of the weather was, and then we said, well, they'd compare pretty well. Greensville and here, the prairies would come out about the same. We'd get snow and winds drifting, so it comes out the same thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that the winters really were a lot harder than in the old days?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, I should say so. Down the ranch, we'd just be drifted in solid. And then when we'd get plowed out, it has happened. Maybe the next day, or two or three days, or a week, it's the same old thing. We were just in there solid. Oh, we hate to see the wind come up in the snowing.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: You're betching. Oh, it's terrible.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Our oldest daughter... You talk snow to her, she's ready to fight. She just hates the snow. Poor thing, she has such a time going to school, and that hard winters, you know. So much snow to wade. And so much of the time Frank was in the woods, not even to help her make a trail out to the highways, you know. So she had a hard time with it. Oh, she just hates winter.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I guess it's a lot tougher than now, because it doesn't seem so bad now. She made a remark, the Ohio Valley would remind me of a remark that one of her relatives from Arkansas made years ago. He was talking about the community fence I was in there. That was in the Marks Hotel, the Mrs. Omenszener community.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they were talking different things about the weather, didn't you?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, the only difference between Arkansas and the West is, Arkansas all went with you. No, the dope is it. You left your tracks in Arkansas, but here you take them with you. That dope up there, you know, just keep your stickin', gettin' bigger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The what?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: That dope play on the can opener. Oh, the dope, yeah. Last time you had to unlock the brake blocks out of your wagon, if we used to build up them.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: When it would come, those big snows and windblowing, drifting like that, and it'd just drift the railroad track in. Trains couldn't get through. Yeah, a week at a time. They'd be sometimes two weeks. They'd have to bring the plows up from Lewiston to keep them plowing through. And then they'd get up there to, that's where we see them working there, then. And when they're going to our Grangeville, they have a big something going there just to really cut and throw in that snow both ways. And they'd be without train service there for days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that happen around here too, around Troy? No, I don't think the trains was ever stuck, was they?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, not on the Troy branch, unless it was your railment or something. I want to change the subject and ask you how you met.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How'd you two meet?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, that's a sad story. Let's don't go into that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The sad story? Don't you see?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, it was like a success story to me. Oh, he had worked, I guess, the second year. He had worked for my aunt and met her husband out on the prairie. And then, I guess the second year he was working there, well then, she had little twin girls, and then she had a younger little girl. So even as she had three babies, and doing all the work herself, just a little too much for her, so they got me down there to help her. And that's what happened. Of all the guys there and I, I had to pick that. Boy. And then, then her sister... There was four or five other men working there, because that was the hardest time, you know.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Then her sister came down to the heart place, working there when our old kid was born, and she married Jess Spencer's brother. That was the first house on the left as you go out to Troy. Her sister. That was like a mile and a quarter out of town. Then his son, see, his one of his, you know, one of his sons had the trailer house that was just below him always. Another son lives outside of him there, and the third son lives up in the second house up the road on the left hand side there. No, the first house on the right.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, they are on the left. There is a place that I always have to work out. I'd be all right going to our dairy until I got up there almost to where this one son lives, now where his grandmother and grandfather live.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like who?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: The fellow used to live at Troy. He's dead now. He went back to Fargo, North Dakota, and he's got a son there. He's in the U .S. employment office. So he bought a deep freeze out of bargain there, and had his pickup. He was coming west. He came over a hill doing about 60. He bought too much of a hill in that Dakota country, you know. But he came over there with a new oil there. He said, boy, he's done some careful driving and some big easing up on that trailer for a while. That stuff's pretty slick, and it's new.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear the story about Driscoll getting stabbed in Troy? Driscoll?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, yeah. There's no one of the ... Driscoll's there. He was a stranger. He was coming down. I'd go to the call there. On the hill, that's how the name is. I've heard it was... I know. Shoemaker. Shoemaker, yeah. Kentucky. Yeah, there's a lot of stories attached to that afterwards. But Driscoll was a... This Driscoll, he was a Pullman. I know a little Driscoll from Troy. But he was mouthed. He might not have been a troublemaker, but he was awful mouthed. And he was putting a good jag on him up there. And this girl, he was bothering her up there. So if you're a Shoemaker, step in. And pull him a letter of mine, his own business. That was all right. He did. He took Shoemaker's word for it, I guess. And then Tilden started... After the show, he started down there. Isn't that... Aunt Tilda's Hall, maybe...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what would you say it is today, Roger?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: You know, where the stake aptitude is, the lumber aptitude is rigged up there. Well, that building across their apartment, that was the hall. So Tilden was going down. This guy and some more got in close behind him, and he'd be making remarks about Tilden. And said something that he was afraid of, and talking like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Tilden stopped and turned around, and said, Well, it's you talking to me?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: And Driscoll said something, and he got the knife stuck in his knife. Shoemaker said that he put his hand back like he was reaching for a gun. And some people said he had a gun. And there was no gun found on him, though. And he was down to... I don't know what to be in it today. The old post office building with a couple of doctors upstairs. And he says, Who... my God, who cares for Mother now, he says. And he crumpled down the sidewalk and died.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Some said that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was that, Driscoll?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah. Some said... Some said that Driscoll was kind of making up to this girl. You can't tell, you see, there are many stories.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But Shoemaker stabbed him in the side?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah, the Shoemaker was making up this girl. He'd take the knife and run, and he didn't bother anymore. He had to be a troublemaker, you know. Getting fresh up there with the girls and then following him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Shoemaker?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, well, I knew Shoemaker very well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That wouldn't be any Voskers, would it?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: It wouldn't be any Voskers. No, he was...

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Let's see... His father's up there in the Spencer Cemetery of ours. Well... Well, he was in Kentucky, she was... That was kind of like a case of...

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, it was last summer or fall, up at...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it Kooski or Stites?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: That that guy resigned... Yeah, Stites, the policeman. Well, he resigned from the police force. And then a few hours after, he shot and killed a guy there in town. And so we read all about it, and our granddaughter and her husband were up here... He's Catholic and older. Winter. We was talking about it, and he kind of laughed, and he says, Yeah, so the only thing about that, that should have been done years ago. He says, that guy was absolutely no good. He said he was always looking for trouble, picking trouble. And he says, he finally got it, but he didn't get it soon enough.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah, the police...

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He says, that man wasn't... That man is not to be blamed. He says, he was picking on him, just a picker, and always looking for trouble. He said, that's his life. And he says, well, he won't do it anymore now. This is the man who was killed. He says, he was no good. Troublemaker, always looking for trouble. So he's just nothing but a troublemaker.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well... Well, I've talked about a troublemaker, and I knew it for a couple of years. Well, we both belonged to the other folks, and naturally got pretty well acquainted with it. I couldn't see any reason or anything mean about a troublemaker that way. Someone said, oh, he's from Kentucky, and well, that doesn't mean that they're all that way. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story on Clam getting killed?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: This Clam fella got killed in trouble. Oh, yeah. He had a restaurant in there. No. A nigger had the restaurant. Well, let's see. I believe he worked in there. Maybe he cooked in a restaurant. Anyway, this... The time of this activity's going on, there's a railroad over here, you know, and the field... A couple of niggers come in there, square and lay, and they put in a restaurant, right where Jimmy Arnett's... Ponce it is now. And, of course, there'd be some girls come up from Lewis occasionally, and there was some of them there one night in the restaurant. Next morning, Clam was dead out on the sidewalk. They arrested the nigger for it, and the nigger got it. All he got is to get death penalty in around him. He got quite a sentence. And the slowly keeper there took it up, didn't figure he got a square deal. Got another trial and cleared him. He only had a good job for the town. Damn, Clam was no good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He cleared the Negroes?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah. I heard that Clam drank a lot, and he was sort of a troublemaker. I don't know if he was so much of a troublemaker, but he was just such a useless drunk, and just neglected his family, you know. I never heard of him doing any fighting, and a little bastard couldn't have done anything. He wouldn't have mounted anything any other way. He did drive. But then they could make an awful nuisance in the settlement, regardless of the size, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say these girls come up from Lewis, and you mean they were sporting girls?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah. Yeah, the nigger had split up. There was only one. I think it was the squares, as there was a time. Then the other nigger had sold out and went off. But he had a good rest and done good business there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know how Clam was killed? Was he shot or...?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, I guess he had just hammered him up enough to get him. It sounds like Troy was a pretty... not a wild place, but it sounds like it was a pretty... It had a share of excitement. Yeah, excitement. Well, now to walk the street and be around there, you wouldn't notice it. But then when you look back over it, it's a little different than what it used to be. Yeah, too. But it never appeared that way, you know, when you're living there. I heard that in the early days, a lot of it was lumberjacks coming in from out in the woods coming into Troy. It's been in their money. They never made much noise. That isn't the... maybe some of the native lumberjacks did, but not the trans. That's not their way. It seemed like that they would travel and get along fine. If they do any... celebrate and get in trouble, it's after they're working and have a payday. That's the way that worked. But you never paid no attention. Had any fear of lumberjacks coming or going. I never did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you buy the idea that 9 out of 10 lumberjacks just drank up what they made? Is that...? You buy the idea that most of the lumberjacks, 9 out of 10 of them, would just go off and have a good time and spend all their earnings and come back broke?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Quite a few of them, Danny. A hell of a lot of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wouldn't you think, men that you would figure knew different, too?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yes, it was splendid, men among them. I've known a lot of them. I've put in several years out there. And it's fine to manage it because you want to meet honest and square in every way. But they would do that. I don't know what was the reason. Then they went broke and all and take the license, woke me up and see another lumberjack. Hate him not to get a little money. Some of the saloon talent. I attended a bar in... I'm moving to Canada now. I attended a bar in Grand Forks, D .C., there in the hotel in the Pacific. Grand Forks were down here, out here at Miles CPR station, and out here about a mile was the great Norman station. The city of the town, smelter in there, had a lot of lumber going on. And the puddle was coming there, going on to work. About a few, five or ten dollars, whatever we wanted, get my bottle, go on his way. They invariably came back and squared that. It was as if that certainly didn't take much of a chance on it. If I would come in there one night, I'd come in there and he was drunk. The boss was on shift, chartered to you. And he said, Charlie, I'm back. Maybe he can grab it to you. He was drunk, so anyway, he wasn't a very good human. He was down back and I'm broke. And he said, I'm broke. I didn't pay you your money. Well, that can't be a hell of a way to do it.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: We called him something and didn't like it. Come on out. Make it all right with him. And you read that part and you had there a few part and you had to do it. He said, Charlie, don't have to do that. He's under the weather a little today. He reached over and got it. And he said, all told the devil out and forget about it. But they knew that. They were there right along. There was one case, the guy didn't call up. Of course, he might have done it another time, but he didn't do that. That's why we got drunk and broke before he got there. Well, it's funny. I've heard the same thing that most of the time you could really count on a man. If he said he'd be bar something, he'd pay you back. He'd settle up his debts before he'd go out and get drunk after he got his pay.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: They sound like a real different breed of man than I've ever run into.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did several of them ever see a married man in their camp?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: It was. He was a poor member of the time, give or something, blacksmith. And the rank of father was single man.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: You know, that's the way of the long little places around. I like, if you've ever heard of Elk City, back in Greenfield, Elk City, and oh, somewhat Greenfield, I guess. Or places like that. They used to be toughs and shooting and burping and all of that little places. About that same time with Troy. Well, it's not those kind of people there at all today, you know. It's just peaceful people and the various sound of hearing anything like that. But they had their rough days too.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: At Greenfield, Troy didn't have having a Greenfield.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He was a Mexican. I guess he was Mexican Indian. Anyway, he was part of the Mexican. And his wife was teaching there. She was reading to it when I was a poor one. He was quite the guy to get drunk and mean, you know. He'd get the devil knocked out of him and then you'd come home and he'd flop up on her. So he'd done that a couple of times. And he got the word, Malick was his name, and he got the word. Now don't let that happen again or you'll be attentive to them. Who's only getting to do that. Went ahead and cleaned up on her again. They took her to jail. The crowd come in there. One of mine wasn't even dark yet. This is an old trustee at the jail there. And if you go on about to find your business, they come down the line. I had friends in the next cell room outside where they were scared.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Jack McLean. Yeah, but don't try to laugh and talk at the same time. You can't understand what you're saying. It isn't that funny. You go to your church and I go to mine. Yeah, I guess you could. But anyway, they walked in by us and said, Well, Malick, we come in to square up and you just... We called them sons of bitches or something and turned over towards the wall. They loaded him down. He wouldn't float at all. And them guys got down to him. But this poor devil in McLean, he was in the next cell there to him. He was in there for cattle rustling. I said, If I got nothing, I'm going to tell you that I don't want to be scared like that again. As you know, there's any damn amount of people like to take a shot at me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they hang him or did they sink him?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Just filled him up with lead. They left him in his bank and shot him in the bunk.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: There was 20 men and he had 20 shots in him. They all shot him to his body.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: They were all going to give it to him. And Judge Steele, he wrote a class in our office in the paper about him. He said, Well, that's the result of this thing. I'll give a man a sentence maybe 20 years to life or murder. And two or three years, he's out of jail again. And running around, he says, That's what you get to do with things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about them hanging Ed Hill over in Colfax? Hanging who? Ed Hill over in Colfax?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, that's out of my territory. Because a similar thing happened to that fellow over in Colfax. He got hung by vigilantes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, he supposedly had killed a cop. I don't know if he really had or not. But they hung him.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Some say it was for horse stealing.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, yeah. But it wasn't that long ago. It was around 1900, around then. Yeah, but we got very little news of that country in here at that time. We had the twice a week spokesperson review, you know. Not very many radios and TVs around that day.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: And so about rounding up horses, rustling horses. There used to be a lot of that around Whiteburg and Greenville country. My dad had a horse taken out of the bunch like that one time. And as they took the bunch of horses to pass, my dad was married and he and his mother were living together out on the prairie. And when they tell, it was a whole bunch of horses, you know, going. Maybe he got up and even looked. But there was one of the names that went by. And dad said that he knew that that was Nellie. He knew her name. So he took her in the bunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you know what that old fool done?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: And I told him so too. I said, well, you darned old fool. He signed a pardon for that guy to get out of jail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He signed a pardon?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah. They went around with a petition, you know, to get this Brooks, Crooks, Rook. Rook, I guess, was his name. To get him on jail, he was the leader of it. He was the boss of it and everything, you know. He's the one that got away with dad's horse, a lot of others. And I don't know how it was. Dad said that they brought it to him. He signed it and I said, well, you darned old fool. I said, I'd like to have been there and scratched it off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what'd you do that for?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: The way he was doing. Oh, well, he says.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: It was a real ring. It was a horse -stealing ring they had going. Yeah. Well, now you sent your head for that. The old man had his points, too. He was a taxpayer in Idaho, and it cost an Idaho mother to keep that man. So maybe the old gentleman looked deeper than the ordinary people do, a gentleman like that.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: And his wife stuck up for him, of course, and she just denied that he never stole. He didn't steal horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And his sister -in -law was telling about it and she said, what was her name?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Maddy. Well, I'll call her Maddy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she says, oh, you made that? You're going to talk about that?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: She says, oh, she just gets so mad and tells you all about that he didn't do that, that he wasn't into that. He was a good man. He made money.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I said, he was one of the aristocrats for that, the Camas Prairie country. Cam runs you off this. Well, in the early years, I think a man could be hung for that. In the early years, a man could have been hung for that. Oh, yes, yes. Back before Bill was, got big enough to spend an organ.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, there was a man down in the Whitebird country who was hung for what was after.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do, supposed to have done?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, the best guy that...

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: They hung him on the field. They shot Maddy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, the best guy that shot Maddy can cross the hand. Well, I don't know about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What story is that?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, something about the horse thief they was after. You hear various stories on that, too. Some figure that was wrong, that they bothered him. More figure he ought to be hung, but you've got to shot every one of us.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: But the Monty Mountain there down the Whitebird, you know, just right down...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know Whitebird?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Right down in the hole, you know, all around the hills after him. This man gives him the best saddle horse that he had to try to think he'd get away from him. But they got him. And I don't know whether it's these people's place or not, but anyway, they got him, and they hung him right up the front yard of some people's home. And the woman just about went crazy about it. And he made them come and cut that tree down. Those of them made them come cut that tree down. They think that they would even do that. They couldn't take him out in the woods a little bit. All the trees would go around, you know, or make the scaffold or something, you know. They'd have to take them in their yard and hang him there.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: She just probably would have made it. Well, that kind of people mixed with them, too.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I said, I would have never given him a horse to try to get away. I tried to see where if I had some place I could push him in and hide him. I said, oh, I couldn't bear to do that. And I said, I'd roll him under my bed. I suppose if they searched in the house, they would kind of vent on him.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: You never know what they'd do. They'd probably be pretty excited trying to find him. Yeah.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I'd just look right there and swear I'd never seen that man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you see him?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Now, you get out of here, and I'm all I'm going to shoot you. I don't know part of this. But I couldn't have been able to see where putting him on a horse, maybe he could get back in the bushes and hide someplace. But I don't know him on a horse. Well, that's good target.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever run across any crazy people? I mean, people who were crazy?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, no.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, I haven't. Every once in a while, somebody turns up in the country, in the early days especially, it seems, they'd be just off by themselves and be crazy.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, I don't, I can't, no, I never moved anyone.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I had two experiences with the equation, man. Scared the hell out of me, too. Once I was a kid, that was in the old country. So I felt a day that I knew well, you know, I was just raising that community, lived there. You had this double bar of shotgun. I was on horseback, giving it a foot. Hello, Frank. God, he looked at me. You was some bitch that hadn't had, I don't know, something about some wire or something, you know. No, I've been talking to him. Figured I was up against him. I didn't know him, he just went crazy, you see. So I got out there alive, and I couldn't get in front of that mug house after. He just simply had too much whiskey available for a minute's time and went wrong. But this deal was just a little toy there, where Clems lived.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: The shorty Clem, he's dead now, father of that electric Clem there. He lived at this idle restaurant in the little house on the hill. I had the greater, left there overnight, right there, just past their mailbox in the ditch. He'd been in the insane asylum before, you know, that was my idea of getting me excited. So I was sitting there in the seat of the greater, waiting for the guy to get the engine ready, and he comes along, he has a butcher and I handed out 22 rifles in his hand. God damn you, he says, now I've got you where I want you. Just where I want you. Well, naturally, I remember him being nuts before, you know. I looked at him and said something, and then he said something else, and he would go home and help somebody butcher, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But by the God of that butcher and I had a rifle, what else would you do if we'd get scared? Who is this now? Who is this man?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Shorty Clem. He bent on the insane asylum. Yeah, I would have been scared too. God damn you know I've got you where I want you. He's just sitting there unarmed. That is a comparison to me with a wild man, Alfred. Yeah, yeah. That's right. He sort of liked the same thing, you know, with thinking something else in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the time that, what did you hear about the man hanging in the canyon?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, it was just from the boys that buried him. They went down there. Somebody reports this body hanging in the canyon there and reported, and here you know the sheriff. The sheriff comes down and he hires these two men, that's Carl Peterson and Tom Christie. They were boys then, you see. Probably 1820, maybe a little older. He hired them to bury him and they took a half gallon of whiskey and they went down and buried him. Now, nobody knew this man that would admit it anyhow around there. Nobody knew him. He found their home. And they went down and they got, Tom said we got drunk in hell and we got $5 a piece. And that was all right. A while after that sometime, by gosh, somebody happens down in there and he finds the grave empty. It opened. And there was a family living down in there. I don't know who they was, but the family living there used to be a road down there. Of course, there was still a road, the county road. This is in Camps Canyon down there. But anyway, the family living down there and they said they'd heard a wagon come in and go out one night. But this man had disappeared. And nobody knew, they would admit it anyhow, who he was or anything. Nobody knew why he was hanging there in the first place then. Well, there was some tough things through there. Quite a lot of horse -tealing was done there in the early days. That's why some people had ideas, but they wouldn't say too much for sure, who they was, you know. But they didn't want to say too much about it.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: You know, he'd been there this evening. They stole horses and they were back in the woods like that. But if anybody ever found out, Clarence Anderson would come to New York, Clarence Johnson, to tell him any more about him than anybody there today anymore. Now, there was a stranger found dead, a man buried, and then they'd feel him taken away. Somebody knew him all right and knew where he was.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He'd probably say, oh, shit, sorry. I really don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is the man just swear?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, shit, sorry. I don't know.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Just like the old fellas killed up there, so some worried about out in the cemetery in Burns region, a log cabin there. Nobody knew who'd done that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Clarence would know much or more about that than anybody. Since Canyon reminds me there of the Olsen place. Since that's the Olsen place right near there, the Olsen place. Yeah, the OK Olsen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, would you tell me that story again about OK Olsen in that place?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: About what I told him about the house. Well, he'd come out talking to me. He'd been in town talking to somebody and said he was digging into me. Well, I didn't put this house. It was up on the ridge. He had a nice farm, 80 acres up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As I said, you know where the machine shed is on Burns Ridge there?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, his place cornered it over there. But OK, he was, he had to, he always had a grin on him. I don't know how to say something to say. Tell him about he was talking to somebody and he'd go on to know why he didn't put the house on Burns Ridge instead of down in the canyon. He said, I will. Yeah, and he said, you know, your idea and my idea was two different things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So why the hell didn't you tell him the truth, OK? Why is that?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: You're the goddamn fool. Oh, that was just laughing.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Now he'd be about to go. She was a jolly here, but she's a pretty jolly girl. His daughter Nellie Johansson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it's that scene, aren't you, Don? So he was a good humor guy, OK Olsen?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Never made him any better. Another time, because of widening that road on the hill above there. Lots of good side of rocks out there. Big man was the cat that straddling on there. He was getting away. The cat to roll him on. OK happened to be on the porch out there. They started working with me. He didn't know OK so well. He'd roll him around and whang. The wire went like a wire head. OK, hey. And he began to laugh.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: The guy got interested a little while ago. The old man laughed. No, he was swell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the rock hit?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: It was a wire fence. He hit the counter. Of course I told him, I said, we're going to roll a lot of rocks down on you, Mother. We'll come in this road and we'll shoot them off out of the way. That's all right, Egypt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: OK, how did he get those initials? Were those just his initials or was that just a nickname?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh no, Olly K .O. Just like the Johnsons had a family with E .K. John. All of them was K in the middle there. I suppose that was Knut.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Johansson? Did you know him at all, the father pair Johansson?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I just knew him by sight. He didn't talk English, you know. All he could do was snore in English.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Funny, this man's business head that he had on belonged to, lived here, isn't he?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Never took on English language, depends on somebody else, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know anything about how they started that, how that brickyard got going there?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, that was his work.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He had the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you mean that on an OK place?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, I meant the one in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's one on OK place, too?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He means in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, this was a little mine there, though K's wasn't it?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He talked about Johansson. Yeah, I'm talking about the brickyard in town.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, he says he's talking about the brickyard in town. Oh yeah, Johansson was the originator of that. He had a little brickwork at home up there. And he got some money together. They used to put each other on Sunday as a manager, another Sunday as a bookkeeper, and got some money going. Hard a man, built up that brickyard. Now they... I forget the name of the fellow, I knew him, but he wasn't there long. But this fellow came in there, and he built it up. He put up those big kills, like, you know, you don't do that in 24 hours, and built the plastic, built that... It's... It feels like a warm view, but there's two kills. There's a division in from there. Well, the number of something you've got to build is, underneath you've got a... Well, the man crawls through it. You've got to crawl through the work in there, and get the sand and ashes out of there. But you've got that bottom in there, and the ventilation, and everything to build is a big job. It costs money. So they got this management there to build the plant. And of course, the... The shareholders ended up... They deceived them when they go home, but they couldn't see no money coming in. Well, of course, they had to get a plant built before they could do anything, you know. Anyway, they got mad, and they fired this foreman, and decided they'd hire another foreman, and they probably forgot that Johansson's out of there, too. They did, and this scotchman from there, Watson. Well, he was bragging about what he done. There were 7 ,200 in debt when he come there. Well, hell, of course there were 7 ,200. These days, they'd be 72 ,000 in debt, you know. Then they'd go, well, all he had to do was to make his brick and burn them and sell them, you know. And he'd brag about what he done. I think it's that sweet, they had another year out, or it might have been better than Watson did. But it satisfied them, they got out of it. They got their money out of it, all right. But they don't think he ever had a chance, you know, to tell them that he was no good with Watson, and they'd Johansson hired him because he could talk sweet.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Then that scotchman that they hired, when they were Watson and Brickmaker, he got in quite a mess there. This is Tom Christie, the secretary of the outside of the lodge, one of the fellows that buried the man in the canyon. He called me in one day, and he said, I got a hell of a letter today. I said, you know, I was working at the Brickmaker, and there was a stock over or something. I stick it in Reddit, and this letter went on, says, I was at a lodge meeting in Vancouver, B .C. a while ago. I think a talking fellow there says, I used to know my J .B. Watson, he said, I had a relation of yours. I said, well, you know. And he says, I think I'm a very close brother to this mother. Well, then he goes to raise the secretary of the outside of the lodge in Troy, that was this Christie, he gets to learn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Christie said, what are you making of that?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I went on a tell, and then about so many years ago, my father left me and my mother and me and my two sisters, and we disappeared, we thought him dead. So he wrote to, first he wrote to Clayton Washington, the court of the general manager, you know, that's where this other man met him. Then they told him that he went to Troy, Idaho, and then he writes the secretary of the lodge there, you see. So he wrote another letter there and said something about him, and he says, he'd like to have us, like, we could get a specimen of his handwriting, and he says, you write it like my uncle used to write it. And just as it happened, had an order in my pocket, he just tore a sheet of paper and had an order in my pocket for something, the stuff they wanted out of the store, and all the hell, send him that. I know what to get, send it back to him, and I guess if you did, the young fella, he'd come along there. He'd come to town and he'd ended up Christie. Him and Christie were walking up there, and he'd taken the young fella up to the plant, you know, and it meant him coming down. So the young fella said, hello, dad, or something like that. And he's kind of, he took him by surprise, you know. He said something to him, finally he says, and first he got sassy, he said, well, prove it, prove it, prove it, prove it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then before, a little bit before they then begin to reply, he says, is your mother and sister still alive?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He was really a big one, you see. The other one was a lot of the Scotland. And her sister, bookkeeper, she stayed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: His wife's sister?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: But you didn't tell quite all that, you know. The old man and this boy, they got together and the father and son, then they wanted to get from Tom something.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: The letters that the kid wrote him. Yeah.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Then they'd have something to come back on Christie about, you know.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Christie said, oh, no, that's fine. Yeah, he took him big with the old man and kind of cold -roared Christie. Christie thought that... He wanted to get these letters that he would send. You got them letters that I wrote to him, it doesn't like to happen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You go to Hamlet, you just start shooting at me, what comeback could I have?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: So the kid forgot about his mother and his sister in Scotland.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He found Papa and they figured out how they could sue Christie in the lodge, maybe.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, boy, he was the old man who went after Christie, if he could.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was he mad at Christie? Because Christie had put the sun on to him?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah, because Christie was... writing truthful answers to his letters and all the harm he'd have.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Him and Christie didn't get along anyhow. Christie was stuck over here. He didn't like him.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Somebody was talking one time, saying, that's damn funny how Rome stays up there, I guess, for once. He was good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Martin said, don't you know how he gets lost?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No. Well, I said, every time Watson comes to you, he starts to swear, Watson don't like to hear his words.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He was quite a rough old Scotsman, he's quite a fella to dig you. But if you dig him back, it was all right, you know. But if you're so cold, and you can get away from him, boy, he'd really ride you. I was coming in one day, I brought something in that was cold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And there's those coming out, fellas that tend to kill, they told me, would you help me carry that door back in there?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I said, yep. So I went back in with the door, and then I was coming out, the old man was coming along, and you'd have to look over the gases.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And who was this coming from?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He was quite glugged on me, he said. I got him, too. And who was... I said, it's me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And who was me?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: The bad guy you hit now, you'll find out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he started to walk off the engine, well, can't you work enough to keep warm without coming in here to warm in the kills?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: And by God, Mr. Watson, I got plenty of good tools. I told him that. He worked to keep warm. And I got him, got him, and away he went. I was getting back at him like that, but it was all right. You see, a fellow, I'm warning him, as he said, this fellow said afterwards, a sun crisp. And he said, you know, I used to be a saloon chipper. I was a saloon for six years. And all the nasty answers that a man could make, I heard them there, and I remember that.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: This sun crisp was doing something there, and Watson come along, and he wanted to talk. He grabbed his teeth out of the oven with his hands. He sounded good -looking. He says, guys, look at the old son of a bitch, got his teeth in his hand ready to bite somebody.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Why, Watson says, oh, good, good. I didn't think it's anything like that to me. Say, you old son of a bitch. Boy, you were just a different girl.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Watson, this guy kind of cringed on him, you know, he just riding with me sometimes. That's the habit he had. The man that he could ride, he'd ride him, but if he didn't, then he wanted me. He was all right.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah, you're a great maker, all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the bank? What about the bank of Troy? You know about the early days at all, the banks and how they made out in the early days?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, I don't know, there was two of them there. It used to be one where the post office is, too, you see, in the early days. But I guess he got along on that. I never knew of any trouble with him. Ekman was the head of the bank across the street at one time where the post office is. He said, his wife worked in there. I don't know. The bank showed up. That's how they credit. Ekman taught school, you know. It's not a long time. He wanted to garnish you somebody else, even to be a month of just mentoring. He wanted to garnish you somebody's bank account that had to go over the money. He said that Mrs. Beckman was the cashier there at the time. He said, I thought it was a good time. I knew I couldn't get the information out of him. I thought he'd get it out of her. He actually said, the soldier who got the bank account, he said, you present his check for payment and find out. He wasn't so good about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Ollie Bowman very much?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, yeah. I knew him. Sam was in him, my dad and my brother. He was a fine fellow too. And Big Axle, his brother, he was like O .K. Olsen. He was cut up. He left Big Axle, only he was quieter.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: You heard of Brockie and how he extended the business of the bank. Well, now, he didn't originate that. I suppose Axle most likely did. Totally Axle. He was more enterprising than Ollie was. But Frank just extended it to me and really went to work on it, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was sort of trained by Ollie, wasn't he?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Ollie, I just came in there. You just came in there, a high school guy, you know, a young fellow.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah, to see him. Of course, we haven't seen him for a long time. But do you remember him when he first came there, he just noticed a kid. And now he's getting to be an old man and has been sick. And then this bank deal, them and kicked him out of the bank, it was been a terrible hard thing on him.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He would soon be able to take a hand on that bank of Juliette when we'd get there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, didn't you read about that the other day? No, did he get it?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, it looks like they're not going to get it there. They don't have enough money. They just don't think it would go there. So the guy said they're going to try and raise more money and then see how it went. I don't know if you asked the other night if you saw that. I saw that.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Frank told John the short one, they expected the short of any day, that is, to have this university lawyer. Oh yes, but this is a... Well, this is since then, I've seen that. Listen, that's about a month later now that the bank, or some bank head that sees the, you know, bank can't go in or not. I figured it would detract from the business of banks and they didn't mention Lewiston, but mentioned Genesee and Troy. But hell, they deserve a bank down in that part of the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that bank and the policies kept to help keep Troy going good in the Depression? Do you think they did much for the town in the hard days and in the earlier days?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, at this point I don't know what they'd done. I never heard nothing far against it, but they did much about it. They'd done all they could do. Because if Frank wasn't doing it, all he was were excellent. And they were the three of them were that kind of men. Now, an example of what Frank done in the small spring was a fellow working on WPA there. I was a foreman, I had a WPA gang because I was highway foreman and things. And we had a fellow there that Frank had been working on it. He was out there and some of them were no good to work. But his brother was shot down in Ontario, Oregon and burned up in his shack. They said his shack was on top of him. And he wanted to go down there and look on to Frank. His brother was interested in it. He thought he probably had something he might get since he wouldn't have gone down there. He'd pay him in a desk store. He went to Olie and told him what he wanted. He wanted him to get just a dollar. Now, mind you, he was not relieved. He didn't have a good job. And he only let him have it, no security. So, you see, Rocky hadn't got nothing out of what Olie had. And I know darn well with Axel was just a good a man's either one. Just as friendly and as generous a man.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, Rocky, he followed along something on that too. Like at the time the bank was the first old draw he'd bank hold up down there. A year, not too many years after it, Frank loaned money to this guy. Frank was the one that the guy come up to the window to the girl there to give him the money. And it scared her so she faded. So he told Frank to come up and give him the money. So Frank had to come up and hand him the money and the way he went and got away. Well, they caught him for too long. And yeah, he must have went to prison for a while.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Anyway, a while afterwards he comes to the bank to borrow some money. After he'd done his time in better country.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Frank let him have it.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, you know, he shouldn't have let him have it. For this reason, his judgment was damn poor. That man had never been caught if he'd been halfway into the business. But he goes right to Spokane. He'd trade his car to enter $800 on a Dodge car. Well, he laid around. He was an all good bum around the Fernwood over there. And he'd pay a bill here and he'd pay a bill there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And buying this and that?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Just jokingly, a guy at the top of it was, Warren Hill, he'd get the money. He said, Warren, you're doing nothing. Just a joke. All the officers, my God, maybe he'll help the back Troy. That's a good look at... If he'd just kept on the bum like he was, you know, solved with that money, he was all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he admit it right there that he'd held up the bank? Did he say it himself?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, they picked him up and they held him. He got his locker in the back of his car. A lot of the money was back there. I guess he didn't deny it. I don't know. But he turned himself in, that's all.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He'd come in with his dinner bucket. The second working man would come into the bank for a little business, you know, which he did. Put his dinner bucket up there and told this girl to put all the money in that. She fainted and told Frank to come up to Phillip. And he'd be beat it off out and there was nobody got the car number or couldn't describe him enough so they could figure out who it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that, the way they called him up there at Fernwood, just like a bunch of them would be standing out here in the yard or us sitting here talking and, well, where in the world does he get that money?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, maybe he's the one that robbed the Troy Bank. That's what the mother said. Just a shot in the neck. Say, this is joking, but it might not be such a joke. That just might be what happened. So, whatever they did after that, we don't remember. But anyway, they went on that and they called him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about that guy that paid to have a saloon burnt down in Troy?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about the guy that paid to have a saloon burnt down in Troy?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Knudsen or there was a guy named Knudsen or Hennings was a guy, he paid this guy to burn his saloon down. Oh, yeah, that was way back too. It was like we've got a corner building burnt there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See, what's wrong with our ears and stories now?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: You know, somebody hard and somebody will burn that out. That is another thing that Steele brought up after Malik was out in Grangeville. Because two or three years after he burnt that building, he confessed to it, he burned the building, he was out in the Marshall and Colley Act.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: See how things went in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You see how many trusting people there were at the same time as in Crooks?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, boy, that seems like they wouldn't go today. But they had quite a life about this guy. He made Frank put the money in the dinner bucket for him and escaped. Then he comes back and borrows money from him. That is funny, that is funny. I suppose Frank, he wanted very few people that would loan money. Yeah. Yeah. They said, they said, that would take Frank Rocky, he would be the only one to do it. And he got back too, they said, that the guy paid it back. He was an awfully good -hearted man. Oh, yes. He's a nice man.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: For a long time, indeed, in Silberton, I believe they called it. The hammer's going all over town, making a new town. The railroad's coming in there. Funny thing too, the contract, the main contract was left to John Tour, it was 20 miles down the river from there. You come in the trail down the train, about 2 o 'clock in the afternoon, the mail came up about 5 in the evening. This contract hadn't been re -lefting that, you know. But I was in the barbershop. I don't know if that trip had been, or I was going into your home state. And when I got in there, I wouldn't have taken a whole damn county without a guy to the home state. I was into cold. I was in there and in the barbershop. I'd come in there, and I guess if a marshal was in the chair, and you'd come in there and he'd call the marshal to do something. He'd arrest somebody or something. He'd pull his gun out of there. That's what he said. He was there, and he was so damn shaky in that big gun. He made me kind of shaky too. After a while, then they talked to the barber, and the marshal talked to him. I said, well, the marshal went out. I don't know about it. He was going to make a marshal go to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where's Silverton now?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Well, it's on that branch that went in from Vail, Oregon. You know Oregon, because, well, when that road went up there. It must have been 1913 -14 along there. But the railroad was done when the contract was let to John Tour, if that's 20 miles below there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See, where did that road go from there? Did it go to Burns?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that road from Vail swing on through into Burns?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: It could well be that one. Well, I was in Burns. There was a long way through the railroad. Canyon City, I guess, was John Day. That's the railroad nearest approach when I was there first. And I had eaten nine, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any run -ins with the IWWs, the Wobs, Wobblies?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, they were just the right to rung of lumberjacks there.

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: They had, they said, it's supposed to be a union, you know, the communist act. It's a union. You had to join it if you worked the woods there. I wasn't in the woods then. I was at home and farmed there. But they had this big guy, W .W. Mutney, who paid a dollar, and he was a union man. They had them in there. They took quite a lot of fellas who wouldn't join. They had a bullet paint up here somewhere. I don't know where it was. We lived on the creek there on the main road to enjoy a dairy. You'd see them going through 10, 15 men in a truck. And all of a sudden, he'd go by. He's happy. He's a husband. I didn't. There's one fellow there that used to go around a bucker. As a bucker, the man that saws the loan, you know, he didn't want to work there, but he'd carry his falling board with him. He'd carry the horseshoe fairs that made it. He'd nail it on here, and then the corg. And there, when you cut a notch into your tree, it'd be like that. You'd take that springboard in, you'd get up on it, and saw your tree. It takes a little practice. I'd rather do it, like riding a bicycle. But you had to go to work for the company. There was no chip warmer than go to work by the day. So he says, well, I knew other people that went out. And T .P. Jones come along by. There was a superintendent there. T .P. come along by where he was working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, what does this captain down 4Ls pay anyway? So the 4Ls get to her, what does this job pay anyway?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Jones is a little out of humor. That's what the 4Ls schedule is on the door in there, the office. To hell with the 4Ls. I'm working for the government. But now I'm in the road, you're a cranky son of a bitch. But you want your round here. It's up to what he wants. You ought to get fired. Then he gets you to want it. Yeah. 4Ls get you down the door. So there's a little excitement with them. Nobody's doing it very seriously. They never got nowhere to rid it. And they did two away with the blankets and got to eat our day, though. They've probably done that much. It's 10 hours and pack your own blankets before that. So another thing that's required is to keep the hogs pinned up around camps. They really wanted their blankets, harnesses, and sheets on beds. So they did something good. Oh, you bet you liked it. They give them a tough name. They were just ordering a row of lumberjacks. Nobody was afraid of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anything that went up in smoke, guys? Well, that was a wobbly's, wasn't it?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Yeah, Harrison, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the same thing?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Just happened to go to a different town, the D of our Harrison. Oh, it was the Howard. Send us out the militia. The wobbly's weren't up our town. The wobbly's didn't burn your town. Here's a hundred dollars' standard relief. Huh. It was some government. The governor happened to be right there in town at the time. He'd come across the lake on the boat, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The governor had a hundred bucks? The governor of Idaho, is there at the time? Was the governor getting a hundred bucks?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: He was getting a hundred bucks at the start of the start relief.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he blame the wobbly's, the governor?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: No, no. He told them something else was part of the wobbly's. He knew it wasn't a wobbly's. So they pinned a lot on the wobbly's that they'd never done. Oh, sure. They hadn't been here. They were arresting men. He came and six fellows on old man who was in there at the time. He said they came in there, he jumped up, took his hands out in the air. There's a lot of them. That's all right, Dad. Put your hands down and sit down. That man's got a gun. I'm scared. It's like heads in the air. He's just making fun of them, you know. That man's got a gun. I'm scared.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever know Dick Farrell or was he after your time? Did you ever know Dick Farrell or was he after your time? That preacher?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh, that now. He couldn't breathe through my dog, too. You saw him then. Yes, I saw him in the early lecture. Dick Farrell. I was wondering if it couldn't hardly be him and Dick Farrell preaching around Lewis and Clarkson now. No, he's dead. That's always, I figured that he, anyway, he should. So he was a joke. He really wasn't as much good as a preacher. He couldn't even talk, you know, how to talk. He was just talking there and I had a car, you know, had a bat in it, had several tables, magazines, writing material in there. And he'd come in the election one Sunday, but he couldn't even talk about something about being here. Or you might get hurt, he says.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He might get hurt?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Hurt it. Hurt it, you know. You know, the kind of preaching line. I don't know what to tell you. Whoever got him on the move, started him into that, or whoever got a siege. Didn't have a hell of a lot to do. You know, there's a Dick Farrell, he's a minute, you know. As he mentioned in the Lewis and papers, I was wondering if that damn fool's still alive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it Chuck like when you were in the camps? Was it pretty good? Chuck?

speaker: Frank or Lottie Rowan
words: Oh God, yeah, she bet you. And yet some of them kick up. Yeah. I was talking to one fellow about one time. I was going to school then in a pre -generational building, five last Thursday.
